steps.voice: Most of the time you don't need this — pick your ECU from the wizard's preset list and the dash decodes everything for you. But if you're on a custom protocol, an OEM ECU not yet in the presets, or you want to add a sensor outside the standard set, every widget can be wired up by hand. Long-press any widget to start.

Config modal. Open the CAN Signal tab — that's where the manual fields live. Underneath the preset picker there's an Expand CAN Bus Settings panel; everything below is what the firmware actually decodes a signal with.

CAN ID. Which message on the bus to listen on. Hex or decimal — the dash accepts both, so 0x218 and 536 are the same address. Your ECU manual lists these. If you don't know, a CAN sniffer like SavvyCAN with a USB-CAN adapter can log raw traffic and tell you which IDs your ECU is broadcasting.

Bit Start and Bit Length. A CAN message is just eight bytes of data — sixty-four bits — and your signal is a specific field packed somewhere inside them. Bit Start says where the field begins, zero through sixty-three. Bit Length says how wide it is. Sixteen bits is a common width for things like RPM or coolant temperature.

Endianness. Intel is little-endian, Motorola is big-endian. ECU vendors don't agree, and this is the single most common reason a value reads wild — sixteen-thousand instead of sixty-four, or bouncing negative when it shouldn't. Nine times out of ten, just flipping this fixes it. Worth trying first before you doubt the rest of the params.

Scale and Offset. The decoded value is just raw times scale plus offset — a linear transform. RPM packed as a sixteen-bit integer often comes through with scale zero-point-twenty-five, no offset. Coolant temp typically scales by one with an offset of minus forty so the dash reads degrees Celsius directly. Whatever your ECU manual says, copy it in literally.

Save and the layout reloads with the new signal bound. If you want to skip the hand-entry entirely, Studio's DBC Import takes a vendor-supplied dot-d-b-c file and converts the whole thing into RDM signals in one shot — find it in the hamburger menu inside Studio. The Marketplace also hosts community-contributed signal packs, so someone may have already done the work for your car.
